title: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory Outlines Quantum Industry and Computational Infrastructure Partnerships - Quantum Computing Report
gist: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory (Berkeley Lab) is integrating commercial quantum systems—QuEra's Aquila and IBM processors—into its NERSC HPC infrastructure via the Quantum Computing Access program, while running a 5-kilometer distributed quantum network testbed (QUANT-NET) connecting ion-trap nodes. The lab also transfers hardware configurations, including the open-source QubiC control system used by NVIDIA's NVQLink, to the commercial ecosystem through the DOE's Quantum Systems Accelerator.

insights.claim: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory's NERSC Quantum Computing Access program provides HPC users with access to quantum processors from QuEra Computing and IBM, enabling quantum simulation, chemistry, materials science, and condensed matter physics workloads alongside classical systems.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: NERSC integrating commercial quantum processors from two distinct hardware approaches (neutral-atom and superconducting) signals that national labs are becoming key early customer channels; QuEra and IBM gain access to the HPC user base for applied benchmarking.
insights.quote: the Quantum Computing Access program provides researchers with access to quantum processors from QuEra Computing Inc. and IBM

insights.claim: Berkeley Lab operates the QIS @ Perlmutter program, which integrates QuEra's neutral-atom Aquila platform and its internal magneto-optical trap array alongside classical supercomputing infrastructure for quantum-classical hybrid workloads.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Tight integration of QuEra's hardware into Perlmutter's classical HPC environment is an early proof point for hybrid quantum-classical computing workflows; results from this integration will inform commercial data center architectures.
insights.quote: utilizing QuEra's neutral-atom platform, Aquila, and its internal magneto-optical trap array alongside classical systems under the QIS @ Perlmutter program

insights.claim: Berkeley Lab manages QUANT-NET, a three-node distributed quantum network testbed that connects ion-trap nodes across 5 kilometers of fiber optic network using quantum frequency conversion tools to automate entanglement distribution, operated in partnership with UC Berkeley, Caltech, and the University of Innsbruck.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: A real operational 5-kilometer quantum network testbed with ion-trap nodes and automated entanglement distribution is a meaningful infrastructure milestone; the international partnership with Innsbruck indicates cross-border quantum network standardization efforts.
insights.quote: operates a three-node distributed testbed connecting ion-trap nodes across 5 kilometers of fiber optic network to automate entanglement distribution using localized quantum frequency conversion tools

themes.theme: Technology Transfer from Federal Labs to Industry

insights.claim: The DOE Quantum Systems Accelerator at Berkeley Lab transfers hardware configurations, including the open-source QubiC control system, to the commercial ecosystem; QubiC is actively utilized by NVIDIA's NVQLink platform.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: NVIDIA's NVQLink adopting the federally developed QubiC control system illustrates a direct technology transfer pipeline from national labs to leading commercial platforms; this could give NVIDIA a head start in quantum control software.
insights.quote: the Quantum Systems Accelerator (QSA)—directed by Bert de Jong in partnership with Sandia National Laboratories—transfers hardware configurations to the commercial ecosystem, including reconfigurable atom arrays and the open-source QubiC control system utilized by NVIDIA's NVQLink.

insights.claim: Berkeley Lab's Molecular Foundry and Advanced Light Source deploy automated QIS cluster tool systems and light beam spectroscopy to analyze quantum states in superconducting and topological material structures.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Spectroscopic characterization of superconducting and topological quantum materials at national lab scale accelerates material discovery for next-generation qubit substrates, benefiting the broader quantum hardware supply chain.
insights.quote: deploy automated QIS cluster tool systems and light beam spectroscopy to analyze quantum states within superconducting and topological structures

insights.claim: Berkeley Lab's Advanced Quantum Testbed and Quantum Systems Accelerator manage training pipelines for early-career technicians in cryogenic engineering, hardware design, and software compilation, supported by the QCaMP program that introduces high school educators and students to quantum physics.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Systematic K-12-through-technician workforce pipelines tied to state-level quantum integration programs like Quantum California indicate that California is positioning for long-term quantum talent leadership, which will affect where quantum companies choose to headquarter.
insights.quote: These structures are supported by the Quantum Computing Mathematics and Physics Summer Camp (QCaMP) program, which introduces high school educators and students to fundamental physics concepts to supply technical personnel for state-level integration frameworks like Quantum California.
